betterquesting:10.desc:8:
§6Coker Oil§f

§aThe heaviest coker output — a polluting liquid best fractioned further rather than burned directly!§f

Coker Oil is a flammable, polluting, viscous liquid produced as the primary output when coking heavy petroleum products in the §bCoker Unit§f. Every §e16,000mB§f of Bitumen yields §e1,600mB§f Coker Oil plus §e500mB§f Heavy Heating Oil and Petroleum Coke. It provides §e31.2 kTU§f per bucket, making it a poor fuel — its real value is as a feedstock for the Fractioning Tower.

§6Production§f:
§b• Coker Unit§f:
  §b–§f Bitumen (§e16,000mB§f) → §e1,600mB§f Coker Oil + §e500mB§f Heavy Heating Oil + Coke.
  §b–§f Heavy Oil (§e11,000mB§f) → §e1,100mB§f Coker Oil + Coke.
  §b–§f Industrial Oil (§e7,900mB§f) → §e790mB§f Coker Oil + Coke.
  §b–§f Engine Lubricant (§e12,000mB§f) → §e1,200mB§f Coker Oil + Coke.
  §b–§f Heating Oil (§e1,600mB§f) → §e160mB§f Coker Oil + Coke.

§6Uses§f:
§b• Fractioning Tower§f (§e100mB§f Coker Oil → §e30mB§f Cracked Oil + §e70mB§f Heating Oil). This is the recommended processing path — it converts the low-value Coker Oil into more useful Cracked Oil.

§eTip§f: Do NOT burn Coker Oil directly — §e31.2 kTU§f per bucket is among the worst fuel values in the petrochemical chain. Route it through a Fractioning Tower to recover Cracked Oil and Heating Oil for better returns!
